short.headline: El Nino is a warning light, not a verdict on the monsoon
short.dek: It roughly doubles the odds of a weak monsoon, yet many El Nino years still finished wet. The Indian Ocean, irrigation and food stocks all soften the blow.
short.body: Since 1950, monsoons during an El Nino averaged 3.2% below the long-period normal, against a surplus in La Nina years. But that average hides a wide scatter: 8 of 26 El Nino monsoons fell more than 10% short, while 2002 saw a 20.9% deficit with barely any food-price spike. Whether a drought turns into a crisis depends on the strength of the Pacific warming, the state of the Indian Ocean, the buffer stocks in government warehouses, and the reach of irrigation. El Nino loads the dice. It does not throw them.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: When the Pacific Ocean heats up in a particular way, it tugs the monsoon engine eastward, and India often gets less rain. Not always, but often. Since 1950, roughly three in five El Nino monsoons turned out below normal. But two in five came out fine. So if you hear 'El Nino' in the news, it means keep an eye on the sky, not that the year is already ruined. The real damage lands on the rainfed crops in the northwest and centre, and on the prices of pulses and onions. But public grain stocks and canals blunt the blow. A farmer in Punjab with tubewells feels it far less than a farmer in Jharkhand waiting for the clouds. The monsoon is still the biggest bet in Indian life, but El Nino is not the last word.
macha.soWhat: El Nino loads the dice, it does not throw them.

article.title: What El Nino Does to the Indian Monsoon
article.standfirst: El Nino tilts the odds toward a weak monsoon, but it does not lock in drought. The Indian Ocean, irrigation, stocks and policy all get a vote. Here is what the data says.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Why look at 125 years of monsoon rainfall?
The India Meteorological Department has tracked every June-to-September monsoon since 1901. That record shows a system that never repeats: a deficit of 13.8% in 1901, a surplus of 7.8% in 2025, and every shade in between. The monsoon delivers roughly 70% of India’s yearly rain, and its long-period average is about 88 cm. But the average is just a number. This chart, as stripes, makes visible the relentless irregularity that sits behind every farmer’s anxiety. Some years the sky opens; some years it stutters. No two are alike. That is the raw material that El Nino interacts with, a baseline of constant surprise.

## How do we track the monsoon's ups and downs year by year?
Stripes are good for seeing the sequence, but a line chart shows how one season’s rain follows the next. From 4.2% above normal in 1950 to the 7.8% of 2025, the line swings above and below the zero mark. Sequences matter: a run of good years fills reservoirs and builds confidence, while a dry year after a good one hurts less. The chart also sets up the El Nino years we will shade later. For now, notice that the monsoon’s tendency is to oscillate, not to stick to any one pattern for long. That is why picking out a single cause is hard.

## So does El Nino make the monsoon drier?
Group every monsoon since 1950 by the Pacific’s state at the time, and average the rainfall departure. In the 26 years when El Nino was active at any point in the season, the all-India rain came in 3.2% below the long-period normal. That is still inside IMD’s ‘normal’ band of roughly ±10%, but it is the driest of the three. Neutral years averaged a 2% surplus, and La Nina years, when the Pacific is unusually cool, a 5.9% surplus. The count of years that fell below zero rain departure tells the same story: 15 of 26 El Nino seasons, against 9 of 27 neutral and only 4 of 23 La Nina. The Pacific tilts the scale.

## How much does El Nino raise the risk of a poor monsoon?
Not every dry monsoon is a crisis. The Ministry’s worry threshold is usually a departure of more than 10% below normal. In the period since 1950, 57.7% of El Nino years fell below normal, nearly double the share in neutral years (33.3%) and more than triple the share in La Nina years (17.4%). For deeper deficits, the gap widens: 30.8% of El Nino years saw a deficit greater than 10%, against 7.4% of neutral and only 4.3% of La Nina. So yes, El Nino roughly doubles the odds of a weak monsoon. But it is not deterministic: 11 of 26 El Nino monsoons still finished above normal.

## Is the El Nino link really as mild as -3.2% rain?
The number that gets quoted depends on the rulebook. The widest count, any El Nino brushing the season, gives the -3.2% average over 26 years. But if you count only those monsoons when the Pacific stayed in El Nino through all four months, the average deficit deepens to -6.8% over 17 years, and below-normal years rise to 71%. Focus on the strongest events, and the average is -12.1% across 7 years. In other words, the bottom-line number is a choice. The El Nino monsoons people remember, 1972 and 2002, are worse than the headline suggests.

## Has the El Nino-monsoon relationship been changing?
In the late 1990s, scientists noted that the Pacific’s influence on the Indian monsoon seemed to be weakening. This chart tracks the relationship as a 21-year rolling correlation between the ONI and monsoon rainfall. The correlation does wobble: it weakened to its weakest near the late 1990s, then tightened again through the 2000s and 2010s. By the 2015 window, the correlation was -0.64, meaning the Pacific’s warming and India’s drying still move together, but not as strongly as in some earlier periods. The lesson: the historical base rate is a guide, not a guarantee, for the current year.

## Is the Pacific the only ocean that matters?
No. Right beside India, the Indian Ocean has its own temperature seesaw, the Indian Ocean Dipole or IOD. When its western half near Africa warms and its eastern half cools, the resulting difference in sea temperatures (measured by the Dipole Mode Index, or DMI) can pull rain into the monsoon. The chart here places the Pacific’s ONI and the IOD’s DMI on the same timeline. They often swing differently. A positive IOD can fight a drying Pacific, while a negative IOD can make a weak monsoon worse. Watching only the Pacific is like listening with one ear.

## Can a warm Indian Ocean offset a dry Pacific?
This chart splits the El Nino monsoons into two groups: those with a positive IOD and those without. When an El Nino monsoon coincided with a positive dipole, the all-India rainfall averaged just -0.3%, essentially normal. Without a positive dipole, the average slipped to -3.9%. The sample is small, only 5 years, but the hint is clear: the Indian Ocean can push back. The textbook example is 1997, a record El Nino with a strong positive IOD, and the monsoon held. Yet it is not a perfect shield; 1972 was a severe drought even with a positive dipole.

## So which El Nino years came out wet?
If the article stopped at the drought years, it would mislead. A large minority of El Nino monsoons finished near-normal or surplus. The top exception was 1958, with a 14% surplus, despite a moderate El Nino and a negative IOD. 1953 delivered a 10.7% surplus, and 1963 a 4.4% surplus. Even 1991, a year of economic crisis, saw only a -1.4% rainfall deviation. These years remind us that El Nino is not a sentence. The monsoon has other drivers, and sometimes they win.

## Which El Nino years brought the worst droughts?
This is the guardrail chart. The worst El Nino monsoons on record are benchmarks against which 2026 will be measured. 1972 stands first, with a 22.3% deficit, followed by 2002 at 20.9%. 1965 lost 18.6%, and 2009 shed 18.3%. These were the years when the monsoon failed badly. 1972 and 2002 also bracket a shift in the economy: the share of agriculture in GDP had shrunk by the 2000s, so the macroeconomic blow was less. But for the farmer in Bundelkhand or Marathwada, the rainfall number on the gauge was the same stark shortfall.

## Does El Nino hit all of India the same way?
No. The all-India number conceals large differences. This chart splits the El Nino years into the four IMD homogeneous regions and shows each region’s rainfall departure as a separate line. The northwest, the wheat-and-pulses belt, often swings deepest into deficit, while the south peninsula can stay close to normal. In 1951, the northwest lost 27.9% while the south lost only 6.7%. In 2023, the all-India number was -5.3%, but the northeast plunged to -17.5% even as the northwest gained 1.2%. India does not eat an average.

## Which part of India loses the most rain?
Averaging the 17 El Nino monsoons where the Pacific stayed warm all season, the northwest lost 14.2% of its typical rain, nearly double the all-India figure of -6.8%. Central India lost 9.4%, and the south peninsula 7.4%. The northwest is the most monsoon-dependent for its kharif crops, so this extra dryness matters for pulses, cotton and millets. The pattern arises because El Nino weakens the monsoon trough and the rain-bearing systems that travel up the Gangetic plain into the northwest.

## When in the season the rain goes missing
In El Nino monsoons, the rain shortfall concentrates at the two ends: June, the onset month, runs 10.3% below a typical June, and September, the withdrawal month, runs 10.8% below. July is a bit better at -6.1%, and August holds up best at -2.7%. A weak June means sowing is delayed; a dry September saps the final fill of the grain. The middle months can partially recover, but a late start often sets the crop behind. The monthly chart explains how a season with a modest overall deficit can still damage the harvest, if the rain is missing when it is needed most.

## Where El Nino actually cuts the rice harvest
The rainfall map tells one story; the harvest map tells another. This choropleth shows the average change in kharif rice yield during El Nino years, measured against the crop’s own five-year normal. The damage clusters in the rainfed rice belt of eastern India, Jharkhand, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, where yields typically fall below their recent average. Meanwhile, heavily irrigated Punjab and Haryana often register yields above normal. The all-India rice yield across El Nino years is nearly flat, but that average hides a geography of winners and losers. The rice that feeds the public distribution system is largely irrigated and less vulnerable; the rice a sharecropper in Jharkhand depends on is rainfed and more exposed.

## Why the rainfall map is not the yield map
This chart drives the point home by comparing two crops in the same regions. In the northwest, rice, over 90% irrigated, averaged a 7.3% yield gain during El Nino monsoons, even though that same region lost the most monsoon rain. But rainfed coarse cereals in the northwest, like bajra and jowar, fell 11.8% below their recent normal. Across the rest of India, the contrast is similar but less extreme. Canals and tubewells decouple yields from rainfall. The lesson is simple: the Pacific decides how much rain falls; irrigation decides how much that rain matters for the harvest.

## Which crops El Nino actually hits
Groundnut, a rainfed oilseed, saw an 8.3% drop in yield during El Nino years. Sorghum and pearl millet followed at -7.3% and -6.8%. Pigeonpea, a pulse that often grows on residual moisture, was down 5.1%. At the other end, rice was nearly flat, and sugarcane, which is mostly irrigated, edged up 1.7%. The largest damage is to the crops that drink straight from the clouds, oilseeds, millets and pulses. These are precisely the foods where prices can spike after a weak monsoon.

## Does the monsoon still determine how much grain we grow?
More than seven decades of crop data say yes. The correlation between monsoon rainfall and foodgrain output is 0.71, a strong, positive link. For rice, it is the same 0.71. For oilseeds it is 0.66, and for pulses 0.41. Wheat, which grows in the winter, is weakly linked at 0.37. These numbers mean that when the monsoon does well, foodgrain production rises, and when it falters, production often dips. The connection is not perfect, irrigation, temperature and policy intervene, but the monsoon remains the broad basis of India’s food supply.

## Does El Nino really hurt farm output?
Looked at through the national accounts, the answer is mixed. Real agricultural GVA growth averaged 3.1% per year in El Nino monsoon years, against 3.2% in neutral years and a brisk 6.3% in La Nina years. So farm output does grow more slowly when the Pacific is warm, but the average drag is modest. Much of agriculture, dairy, horticulture, winter crops, is not directly hit by the summer monsoon. And in a big El Nino year, the growth can still be positive, as in 1997. The aggregate hides the deeper pain in specific regions and crops.

## What crops does India have in stock before the 2026 monsoon?
Whether a weak monsoon turns into a food-price shock depends partly on the stockpile. The latest government estimates for the 2025-26 crop year put foodgrain production at 3,765.63 lakh tonnes, rice at 1,540.24 lakh tonnes, wheat at 1,206.57 lakh tonnes and pulses at 274.09 lakh tonnes. Oilseeds are estimated at 430.59 lakh tonnes. These numbers are near records. Large public buffer stocks of rice and wheat, in particular, mean that a single poor kharif is unlikely to empty the shelves. But they do not guarantee immunity from a spike in perishables like vegetables and pulses.

## Does a weak monsoon always push food prices up?
No. The eight El Nino monsoons since 1982 produced post-monsoon wholesale food inflation ranging from near zero in 2002, the worst drought of that span, to 16.9% in 2009, a severe drought, to 23.1% in 1991, a near-normal monsoon year. 2002 saw a 20.9% rain deficit yet barely any food inflation because large public grain stocks and a global commodity lull held prices down. The monsoon is a trigger, but prices also answer to stocks, trade policy and global markets.

## Which foods get more expensive after an El Nino?
Food is not one thing. In 2009, with an 18.3% rain deficit, cereals rose 14.5%, pulses surged 32.6%, and onions jumped 29.2%. By contrast, in 2002, despite a worse 20.9% deficit, pulses fell 5.3% and onions dropped 4.9%. Cereals often move little across droughts, sheltered by public stocks and procurement. The sharp spikes are in vegetables and pulses, where supply is more local and less storable. This is why food inflation after a drought is a story of a few specific items, not a uniform rise.

## Is El Nino the main cause of food inflation in India?
Look at the long sweep of inflation since 1960, with El Nino monsoon years shaded. The tallest peak of headline CPI, nearly 28% in 1974, was an oil shock, not an El Nino. The 1991 spike owed more to a balance-of-payments crisis and rupee devaluation. The wholesale-food line, which starts in 1983, climbs in some but not all shaded years. The retail-food line, available from 2012, sits below the wholesale line where they overlap. El Nino is a contributor, but it is not the master switch. Oil prices, global food markets, public stocks and trade policy all get a vote.

## Why does a bad monsoon matter less for GDP but still so much for people?
Agriculture’s share of India’s gross value added has fallen from over 60% in 1951 to about 14% today. So an identical rainfall miss now moves headline GDP much less. But the share of India’s workers still in farming has fallen far more slowly, from 63% in 1991 to about 42% in 2025. That gap between the two lines is the human heart of the monsoon question. A drought is a small event for the GDP growth rate and a large event for millions of livelihoods. Food still takes a big slice of poor households’ budgets, so the monsoon sets rural incomes and the price of a meal even when it barely dents the growth figure.

## Where these numbers come from
The monsoon rainfall figures come from the India Meteorological Department’s Pune office, which has maintained the all-India record since 1901. Ocean temperatures are from the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s ONI and DMI indices. Crop output and yield data are from the Ministry of Agriculture’s DES and UPAg estimates, and district-level yields are from ICRISAT’s district database. National accounts and wholesale prices are from the Reserve Bank of India’s DBIE. The retail food CPI is from MOSPI. Each phase or anomaly is measured against that series’ own baseline. Correlations are descriptive, not causal. The 2026 monsoon forecast is a live projection, not a completed year.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: El Nino is a recurring warming of the central and eastern Pacific Ocean that shifts global weather patterns and has a well-documented relationship with India’s summer monsoon. This page uses over a century of rainfall records, ocean temperature indices, crop yields, and price data to explain what that relationship really means: El Nino raises the odds of a weak monsoon, but it is not a verdict.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of the Pacific Ocean as a giant bathtub. Usually, trade winds push warm water west toward Indonesia, but every few years those winds weaken and the warm water sloshes back east toward South America. That warmer-than-usual patch is El Nino. It heats the air above it and shifts global rainfall patterns, including India’s monsoon. In India, El Nino is associated with a higher chance of below-normal monsoon rainfall.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This page asks what El Nino actually does to India’s monsoon, so the term appears throughout.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: La Nina is the cool sister of El Nino. It happens when the trade winds strengthen, pushing even more warm water to the west, and cold water wells up near South America. For India, La Nina generally tilts the monsoon toward surplus rainfall.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: La Nina is the reference point for what a favourable Pacific looks like, and its rainfall surplus contrasts with El Nino’s deficit.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: ONI is the thermometer for the Pacific’s bathtub. It measures how much warmer or cooler a specific patch of the central equatorial Pacific is compared to a long-term average. If the ONI stays above +0.5°C for several months, we call it an El Nino. Below -0.5°C is La Nina.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: We use the ONI during the June-to-September monsoon to label each year’s Pacific state.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The Indian Ocean has its own temperature seesaw, the IOD. In its positive phase, the western part near Africa warms up while the eastern part near Indonesia cools. That warm pool next to Africa feeds moisture into the monsoon. A negative IOD is the reverse, and can weaken the monsoon.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The IOD is the second ocean that can offset or amplify El Nino’s effect, which is a central theme of the page.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: For India, the southwest monsoon is the June-to-September rainy season that delivers roughly 70% of the country’s yearly rainfall in a few months. It is driven by the temperature contrast between the hot Indian landmass and the cooler surrounding oceans.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The entire page is about how El Nino affects this seasonal rainfall.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The LPA is the average of monsoon rainfall over a long stretch of years, IMD currently uses 1971-2020. It is the yardstick for calling a season normal, below normal, or deficient. A departure of 10% or more below the LPA is typically considered a drought.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All rainfall departures in the charts are measured against the LPA, so it is the baseline for every drought or surplus mentioned.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Correlation measures how closely two things move together. A correlation of +1 means they move perfectly in the same direction; -1 means they move perfectly opposite; 0 means no link at all. In this page, a correlation of 0.71 between monsoon rainfall and foodgrain output means they rise and fall together most of the time, but not always.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The crop correlation chart uses this number to show how much of India’s harvest still follows the monsoon.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The WPI measures price changes at the wholesale level, where traders buy from producers. It is not the same as the retail CPI, which records what households pay in the market. WPI food inflation can be lower or higher than CPI food inflation because the basket and margins differ.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: We use WPI to get a longer price history back to 1982, but it is important to remember it is not the price the customer faces. A glossary callout explains this caveat.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: GVA measures the value a sector adds to the economy, net of the inputs it uses. For agriculture, it is the output less seeds, fertiliser, diesel and other intermediate costs. As a share of total GVA, it tells us how big farming is relative to the rest of the economy.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: We use agricultural GVA growth to show how farm output responds to the Pacific, and its share to explain why a drought now moves GDP less than it used to.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Showing the ONI and DMI on the same timeline lets a reader compare the two oceans at a glance and spot years when they diverge, which is the central mechanism on a phone screen.
Small-multiple line panels for each region let a phone reader swipe through the geography quickly and see that the same El Nino produces very different regional numbers.

chartExplainers.title: A century and a quarter of monsoons that never sit still
chartExplainers.takeaway: Since 1901, no two Indian monsoons have been identical; the rainfall departure record is a constant wobble.
chartExplainers.detail: Each stripe is one June-to-September monsoon season. Blue means the country got more rain than the long-period normal; brown means less. The earliest stripe, 1901, is dry at -13.8%. The latest, 2025, is wet at +7.8%. In between, the colours flicker without settling into a pattern. A run of dry years can be followed by a wet spell. The monsoon delivers about 70% of India’s annual rain, so even a small-looking departure can have a large effect on soil moisture, reservoirs and crops. This chart is the starting point for answering whether El Nino stands out in that long record.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Before connecting El Nino to the monsoon, the reader needs to see the full range of natural variability.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Blue stripes are above the long-term average, brown stripes are below it. The deeper the shade, the larger the deviation.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not search for a fixed cycle. The monsoon’s natural variability is large and irregular.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Narrow vertical stripes allow the full 125-year span to fit a phone without scrolling horizontally.

chartExplainers.title: The same record as a line, so the swings are easy to follow
chartExplainers.takeaway: Turning the stripes into a line reveals how one year’s rain follows the next, and how quickly the monsoon can flip from surplus to deficit.
chartExplainers.detail: This line traces the same monsoon departure figures from 1950 to 2025. It starts at +4.2% in 1950 and ends at +7.8% in 2025. The line swings visibly above and below the zero line. A cluster of wet years, like the early 1950s, can build up groundwater, while a sudden dry year like 1965 (-18.6%) can catch farmers off guard. The line’s upturns and downturns are the backdrop against which El Nino’s influence is measured. It shows that the monsoon is inherently jumpy, so attributing any single year to one cause is hard.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: A line chart makes the sequence of booms and busts easier to read than stripes, helping the reader see the timing of events.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The horizontal dashed line at 0% is the long-period average. Points above it are surplus years, below are deficit years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse a large swing with a trend. The monsoon does not follow a smooth trend.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use sparse year labels and a wide aspect ratio so the line does not look squashed.

chartExplainers.title: On average, El Nino monsoons are the driest
chartExplainers.takeaway: When the Pacific is in El Nino, India’s monsoon averages 3.2% below the long-period normal, but that figure hides a wide range of outcomes.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart groups all monsoon seasons since 1950 into three buckets: El Nino, neutral, and La Nina. The bars show the average departure for each group. El Nino years (26) averaged -3.2%, while neutral years (27) got +2% and La Nina years (23) got +5.9%. The numbers below the bars count how many years in each group fell below zero. In El Nino, 15 out of 26 were dry; in La Nina only 4 out of 23. The visual makes clear that the Pacific tilt is real, but it also shows that the average El Nino deficit is still inside the normal range, which is why the next chart on extremes is needed.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the most direct answer to the page question: El Nino monsoons are drier on average.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The three bars compare the average rainfall departure for each Pacific state. The numbers below are the count of years with below-normal rain.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The average is not a guarantee. A large minority of El Nino years still had surplus rain.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars are easy to scan on a vertical phone screen.

chartExplainers.title: El Nino roughly doubles the odds of a weak monsoon
chartExplainers.takeaway: 57.7% of El Nino monsoons finished below normal, compared to 33.3% for neutral years.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart shows the share of years in each Pacific category that finished below normal, using IMD’s definition. The El Nino bar reaches 57.7%, nearly double the neutral 33.3% and more than triple La Nina’s 17.4%. It also breaks out more extreme thresholds: 30.8% of El Nino years fell more than 10% below normal, against only 7.4% of neutral years. So the risk of a serious drought is much higher during El Nino. Still, the bar is not 100%, a point the next charts reinforce with exceptions and definition checks.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Risk is a frequency, not a slogan. This counts the actual number of weak monsoons under each Pacific state.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The height of each bar is the percentage of years in that Pacific state that crossed each rainfall deficit threshold.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t treat the percentage as a probability for a single year. It is the historical base rate.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Grouped bars with large labels work well on phones if each deficit threshold is a separate colour.

chartExplainers.title: How you define 'an El Nino year' changes the headline
chartExplainers.takeaway: Defining El Nino strictly doubles the average deficit and almost doubles the share of below-normal years.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart shows three El Nino definitions and La Nina as contrast. The loosest, any El Nino during the season, gives a -3.2% average over 26 years. Restricting to monsoons where El Nino persisted all four months (JJAS ONI ≥ 0.5) yields -6.8% over 17 years, with 71% below normal. Strong El Ninos only (peak ONI ≥ 1.5) average -12.1% over 7 years, with 86% below normal. La Nina years average +6.8%. The bars and the shares make plain that the familiar -3.2% figure is the most generous reading. The El Nino monsoons that people remember are the strict and strong ones.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Readers often hear a single number. This chart reveals that the number depends on a hidden choice of definition.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is the average departure for that definition. The small text gives the count of years and the share with deficits.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume all reported El Nino figures use the same rule. The definition matters.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with clear labels are ideal for a quick comparison on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: Has the Pacific's grip on the monsoon loosened?
chartExplainers.takeaway: The rolling correlation between Pacific warmth and monsoon rainfall dipped near zero in the late 1990s but has since tightened.
chartExplainers.detail: The line shows a 21-year running correlation between the ONI and all-India monsoon rainfall. A more negative number means a stronger link (warmer Pacific, drier India). The correlation started at -0.53 in 1960, weakened to near zero around the late 1990s, and then strengthened again to -0.64 by 2015. The wobble is large enough that scientists have debated whether the relationship is breaking down. For the reader, the practical point is that the historical base rate from 1950-2025 is a guide, not a fixed law. The Pacific’s grip has not vanished, but it is not constant either.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This addresses the scientific debate about whether the El Nino-monsoon link is weakening, using a data-driven rolling window.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line goes down when the Pacific’s influence strengthens (more negative correlation) and up when it weakens.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read a single point as a prophecy. The rolling window is a long-term metric.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A smooth line with a few labelled decades works well on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: Two oceans, not one, set the monsoon's odds
chartExplainers.takeaway: The Pacific and the Indian Ocean often swing in different directions, creating a tug-of-war that shapes the monsoon.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines are plotted together: the ONI (Pacific) and the DMI (Indian Ocean Dipole). The ONI shifted into a warm El Nino state in 1951, 1957, 1965, 1972, 1982, 1987, 1991, 1997, 2002, 2004, 2009, 2015 and 2023. In several of those years, the DMI was also positive, 1972, 1982, 1997, 2015, while in others it was negative or neutral. A positive DMI can add moisture to the monsoon, offsetting a drying Pacific. The chart lets a reader scan for years when both were unfavourable (El Nino with a negative dipole, like 1965) versus years when they opposed each other.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The monsoon’s outcome is rarely a single-ocean story. This visual brings the Indian Ocean Dipole into the same frame.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for years where the red Pacific line is high (El Nino) and the blue Indian Ocean line is low (negative dipole) or high (positive dipole).
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the oceans always move together. They are independent.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Two lines with contrasting colours can be distinguished on a phone if the background is clean and ticks are sparse.

chartExplainers.title: Does the Indian Ocean rescue an El Nino monsoon?
chartExplainers.takeaway: El Nino monsoons with a positive IOD averaged near-normal rain, while those without it averaged a clear deficit.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart splits all 26 El Nino monsoon seasons into two groups: 5 with a positive IOD and 21 without. The positive IOD group averaged just -0.3% rainfall departure, barely below normal. The other group averaged -3.9%. A comparison bar for all La Nina monsoons (+5.9%) is also shown. The sample of positive IOD years is small, but the difference is large enough to matter. The strongest supporting case is 1997, a record El Nino that was counterbalanced by a strong positive IOD, and the monsoon survived. The chart drives home the point that the Indian Ocean gets a vote.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This isolates the effect of the IOD during El Nino, directly testing whether the second ocean matters.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the white bar (El Nino with positive IOD) to the light bar (El Nino without). The further apart, the more the IOD matters.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The sample is small; the pattern is suggestive, not a guarantee for any single year.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Grouped bars with a clear legend work well if the groups are kept to three.

chartExplainers.title: The El Nino years that refused to follow the rule
chartExplainers.takeaway: 11 El Nino monsoons still finished with near-normal or surplus rain, including 1958’s 14% surplus.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart lists the years when El Nino was present during the monsoon but the all-India rainfall departure was near normal or positive. Each bar is a single year, and the colour indicates the rainfall departure: blue for surplus, grey for near normal. The list starts with 1953 (+10.7%) and includes 1957 (-0.5%), 1958 (+14%), 1963 (+4.4%), 1969 (+3.8%), 1976 (+1.9%), 1977 (+3.4%), 1983 (+14%), 1991 (-1.4%) and 1992 (-1.4%). These 11 years, from a total of 26 El Nino monsoons, are the statistical basis for the page’s thesis: El Nino is a loaded dice, not a fixed outcome.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Without this chart, the reader might wrongly conclude El Nino guarantees drought. It shows the important minority of exceptions.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars above the zero line are surpluses; bars near zero are near normal. The colour distinguishes them.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume these exceptions were all due to the Indian Ocean. Some had other causes.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A vertical list of bars with year labels is scannable on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: ...and the El Nino droughts India still remembers
chartExplainers.takeaway: 1972 and 2002 are the two worst El Nino monsoons on record, with deficits of 22.3% and 20.9%.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart lists the ten largest all-India rainfall deficits among El Nino monsoons since 1950. The bars are sorted from worst to least: 1972 (-22.3%), 2002 (-20.9%), 1965 (-18.6%), 2009 (-18.3%), 1987 (-14.3%), 1951 (-13.2%), 2015 (-12.7%), 1982 (-11.4%), 2004 (-9.6%) and 1986 (-8.5%). Each row also shows the ONI and DMI values for that season. The presence of 1982 and 2015, which had positive IODs, reminds us that a favourable Indian Ocean is not a full shield. These years are the cautionary tales.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: These are the benchmarks if 2026 turns dry. They ground the risk discussion in actual historical events.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The deepest brown bars are the largest shortfalls. The ONI and DMI alongside show the ocean states.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are not the only drought years in India, only the worst among El Nino monsoons.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A clean horizontal bar chart with a clear rank order works on a mobile screen.

chartExplainers.title: Same El Nino, very different Indias
chartExplainers.takeaway: The same El Nino can cause a drought in one region and a surplus in another, as 2023 showed.
chartExplainers.detail: This visual splits El Nino monsoon years into the four IMD homogeneous regions and plots each region’s rainfall departure as a separate small line chart. The full set of years is shown, so a reader can swipe through the four panels. In 2023, the all-India line shows -5.3%, but the northeast line plunges to -17.5% while the northwest line rises to +1.2%. In 1951, northwest lost 27.9% while south peninsula lost only 6.7%. The charts demonstrate that a national average is a poor guide to local reality, and that El Nino’s local effect depends on the orientation of the monsoon trough and the path of rain-bearing systems.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: India does not eat a national average. The spatial breakdown makes the risk tangible for different parts of the country.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each panel is a line chart for a region. Compare the height of the line in the same year across panels.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume all regions move together. The monsoon is a mosaic.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Small-multiple panels that stack vertically work well on a phone scroll.

chartExplainers.title: El Nino hits the northwest hardest
chartExplainers.takeaway: Northwest India loses 14.2% of its typical monsoon rain during El Nino years, nearly double the national figure.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart averages the 17 monsoons when the Pacific was in El Nino all season, and shows the mean departure for each region. Northwest India tops the list at -14.2%, followed by Central India at -9.4%, South Peninsula at -7.4%, and the all-India figure at -6.8%. The northwest includes the wheat-and-pulses belt, where kharif crops like bajra, moong and cotton depend almost entirely on monsoon rainfall. This extra dryness is why El Nino often shows up in pulse and oilseed prices before cereal prices. The mechanism is that El Nino weakens the monsoon trough over northwest India.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It ranks the regions by sensitivity, showing clearly which part of the country faces the highest rain risk.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The leftmost bar is the largest deficit. The brown bars grow lighter toward the right.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The ranking is from average over 17 events. Individual years can deviate.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A descending horizontal bar chart is instantly readable on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: When in the season the rain goes missing
chartExplainers.takeaway: El Nino monsoons are driest in June and September, the onset and withdrawal months.
chartExplainers.detail: Two sets of bars compare the average monthly rainfall departure in El Nino monsoons against a typical year. In El Nino, June runs 10.3% below normal, July -6.1%, August -2.7% and September -10.8%. A typical year runs close to zero in all months. The chart reveals a U-shaped pattern: the deficit is deep at the start and end of the season, while August holds up best. For a farmer, a 10% shortfall in June means sowing is delayed, which can reduce yields even if July and August recover. A 10% shortfall in September saps the final grain-filling stage.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The timing of the deficit determines how much damage a given seasonal total does. A dry June hurts differently from a dry September.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the brown El Nino bars to the grey typical-year bars for each month. The gap is the El Nino effect.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume all months are equally important. The crop calendar gives weight to June and September.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Grouped bars by month, with two colours, are compact and easy to read on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: Where El Nino actually cuts the rice harvest
chartExplainers.takeaway: El Nino’s rice-yield damage concentrates in the rainfed belt of eastern India, while yields in irrigated Punjab and Haryana often rise above normal.
chartExplainers.detail: The map shows the average kharif rice-yield change in El Nino years, measured against the crop’s own five-year normal. States in the rainfed eastern and central belt, Jharkhand, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, typically see yield declines, shaded in orange and red. The highly irrigated northwestern states of Punjab and Haryana are shaded green, indicating yields above their recent normal. The all-India rice yield is nearly flat, but that average cancels out the losses in the east against the gains in the northwest. The pattern is driven by irrigation access, which decouples the harvest from the rainfall in the canal and tubewell districts.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It puts the geography of yield loss on a map, making the distributional impact of El Nino concrete.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Green states had above-normal rice yields during El Nino years; red states had below-normal. The deeper the colour, the larger the deviation.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not interpret the map as a prediction for a single future El Nino. It is the average of past ones.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A choropleth map works on a phone if the colour legend is simple and the states are large enough to tap.

chartExplainers.title: Why the rainfall map is not the yield map
chartExplainers.takeaway: Irrigated rice in the northwest gained 7.3% yield during El Nino years, while rainfed coarse cereals in the same region fell 11.8%, proving irrigation is the key buffer.
chartExplainers.detail: Two crops in the same regions tell the story. The top set of bars shows irrigated rice yields during El Nino years. In the northwest, rice averaged 7.3% above its recent five-year normal. But the bottom bars, showing rainfed coarse cereals, reveal a very different picture: in the northwest, they fell 11.8% below normal. In Central India, rice fell 3.1% while coarse cereals fell 1.2%. In the south, both were small positives. The chart makes the case that where canals and tubewells reach, the Pacific’s influence on yields is largely muted. Where they don’t, the rain deficit translates directly into lost harvest.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This visual directly tests the irrigation hypothesis by holding geography constant and varying the crop’s water source.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the two bars for the same region. A large gap means irrigation matters. The numbers are percentages above or below the crop’s own pre-El Nino five-year average.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume all rice is irrigated. In the eastern states, much rice is still rainfed.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Grouped bars by region, with two crop groups, are readable if the colours are distinct and the labels short.

chartExplainers.title: Which crops El Nino actually hits
chartExplainers.takeaway: Rainfed oilseeds and millets suffer the largest El Nino yield losses; irrigated crops like rice and sugarcane are barely affected.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart shows the average yield change in El Nino years for ten major crops, measured against each crop’s recent five-year normal. Groundnut leads the losses at -8.3%, followed by sorghum (-7.3%), pearl millet (-6.8%), pigeonpea (-5.1%) and oilseeds overall (-5.1%). Maize is down 2.4%. At the other end, rice is nearly flat (+0.3%), cotton is up 1.2% and sugarcane gains 1.7%. The pattern sorts by water source: crops that rely heavily on monsoon rainfall lose yield; crops supplied by irrigation hardly move. This explains why a weak monsoon shows up first in edible oil and dal prices.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the crop-level evidence for the irrigation story. It shows the precise commodities where El Nino risk is highest.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars to the left of zero are losses; bars to the right are gains. The numbers are relative to the crop’s own average in the five years before each El Nino.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are all-India averages. Regional averages can differ sharply, but the crop ranking is consistent.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A vertical bar chart with crop names on the x-axis works on a phone if rotated or if the bars are short.

chartExplainers.title: Rainfall still shows up in the harvest
chartExplainers.takeaway: The correlation between monsoon rainfall and foodgrain output is 0.71, meaning they still rise and fall together most years.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart shows the cross-correlation between monsoon rainfall and annual crop output for six broad crop groups from 1950-51 to 2024-25. Foodgrains and rice both register a 0.71 correlation coefficient, a strong positive relationship. Oilseeds follow at 0.66. Coarse cereals and pulses are weaker at 0.51 and 0.41. Wheat, a winter crop that relies on stored moisture and irrigation, shows only 0.37. These numbers mean that while the monsoon is no longer the sole master of the harvest, it remains the single biggest driver of year-to-year swings in total food production. Irrigation and technology have not severed the link; they have loosened it.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This connects the rainfall story to the food supply, using a 75-year dataset that covers the Green Revolution period.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is a correlation coefficient between 0 and 1. The longer the bar, the stronger the link.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Correlation is not causation. But the consistency over decades and across crops points to a genuine physical link.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with crop names on the left are easy to scan on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: Farm output feels the Pacific - but less than you would guess
chartExplainers.takeaway: Real agricultural GVA grows at 3.1% per year in El Nino years, compared to 6.3% in La Nina years, a drag but not a crash.
chartExplainers.detail: Using Reserve Bank of India national accounts data from 1951 to 2025, the chart averages annual real agriculture GVA growth under three Pacific states. In 16 El Nino monsoon years, growth averaged 3.1%. In 41 neutral years, it was 3.2%. In 17 La Nina years, it jumped to 6.3%. The divergence is clear: farm output grows about twice as fast in La Nina years as in El Nino ones. Yet the El Nino average remains positive, reflecting the growing weight of dairying, horticulture and winter crops that are not directly hit by the June-to-September rain. The chart tempers the drought narrative with the fact that the whole farm sector rarely shrinks.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It tests the El Nino-farm output link with the official national accounts, giving a macro measure of the impact.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The three bars show the average growth rate for each Pacific state. The higher the bar, the faster farm output grew.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are averages. In individual strong El Nino years, growth has turned negative (e.g., 2002).
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with large percentage labels are clear on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: The food buffer India carries into the 2026 risk season
chartExplainers.takeaway: India enters the 2026 monsoon with near-record production of foodgrains, rice and wheat, providing a substantial buffer against a weak kharif.
chartExplainers.detail: Based on the third advance estimates for 2025-26, foodgrain production is estimated at 3,765.63 lakh tonnes. Rice is pegged at 1,540.24 lakh tonnes, wheat at 1,206.57 lakh tonnes, pulses at 274.09 lakh tonnes and oilseeds at 430.59 lakh tonnes. Sugarcane is estimated at 5,000.63 lakh tonnes and cotton at 290.24 lakh tonnes. These are near-record levels. The large stocks of rice and wheat in government warehouses mean that a single poor southwest monsoon is unlikely to cause a cereal shortage. The risk for consumers concentrates in pulses and perishables, where public stocks are smaller and supply chains are more local.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This sets the table for the food-price discussion. A drought’s impact on prices depends heavily on the opening stock levels.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The bars show the estimated production for the 2025-26 agricultural year. The longer the bar, the larger the buffer.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are production estimates, not ending stocks. Some part goes to consumption and exports before the monsoon.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A horizontal bar chart sorted by output quantity is compact and scannable.

chartExplainers.title: A drought does not automatically mean dearer food
chartExplainers.takeaway: The 2002 monsoon, the second-worst El Nino drought in the record, saw only 0.9% post-monsoon wholesale food inflation.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart lists eight El Nino monsoons since 1982 and their post-monsoon (October-December) wholesale food inflation. 1991, a near-normal monsoon, had the highest inflation at 23.1%, driven by a balance-of-payments crisis and rupee devaluation. The two worst monsoons, 1987 (-14.3% rain) and 2002 (-20.9% rain), saw food inflation of just 8.8% and 0.9%, respectively. In 2002, large public grain stocks and a global commodity lull kept prices low despite the drought. The monsoon is a trigger for food prices, but stocks, trade policy and global markets are the transmission mechanism. A dry year does not automatically mean dearer food.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This chart tears down the simple drought-equals-inflation assumption by showing the actual post-monsoon price record.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is the three-month wholesale food inflation after that year’s monsoon. The rainfall departure is shown below the year label.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: WPI food inflation is not the same as retail CPI. The CPI series starts only in 2012.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with clear year labels and a secondary rain label work well on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: After a drought, food prices do not move as one
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2009, cereals rose 14.5% but pulses surged 32.6% and onions 29.2%; in 2002, despite a larger rain deficit, pulses and vegetables fell.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart splits post-monsoon wholesale food inflation into four commodity groups for four El Nino drought years. In 2009, all groups rose, but pulses jumped 32.6% and onions 29.2%, while cereals increased only 14.5%. In 2002, cereals were up a muted 3.1%, and pulses, vegetables and onions all posted negative inflation. The 2015 drought saw a 51.6% spike in pulses but only 1.3% in cereals. The pattern is clear: cereals, which are stored by the government, show small price moves in drought years. Perishable vegetables and the less-storable pulses show the sharp spikes. Food inflation after a poor monsoon is a story of a few items, not a uniform rise.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Food inflation is a bundle of separate shocks. This breaks it open to show which foods actually carry the pain.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each drought year has four bars for four food groups. Compare the height of the cereal bar with the pulse and vegetable bars.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are wholesale prices, not the prices consumers pay. The gap between wholesale and retail can widen after a drought.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Small multiples with a common axis and colour-coding for each year can be stacked vertically for phone scanning.

chartExplainers.title: El Nino is one driver of food prices, not the only one
chartExplainers.takeaway: The tallest inflation spike in the record, 28% in 1974, happened in a year with no El Nino, driven by the global oil shock.
chartExplainers.detail: Three inflation lines are plotted from their respective start dates: headline CPI from 1960, wholesale food from 1983, and retail food from 2012. El Nino monsoon years are shaded in red. Many of the shaded years show no exceptional spike; some, like 2002, sit near zero. The 1974 peak, the highest on the chart, was an oil-price event. The 1991 spike was a currency crisis. The 2020 and 2022 spikes were not monsoon-driven. The wholesale-food line is the most sensitive to the monsoon, yet even it does not climb in every shaded band. The visual makes clear that El Nino is only one thread in the inflation story.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The long view is the honest test of the monsoon-to-prices link. This chart puts El Nino years in the context of half a century of inflation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the shaded red bands (El Nino years) and see whether the lines spike. Only some do.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The three lines measure different price baskets and time periods. They cannot be directly compared to each other for a given year.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A multi-line chart with sparse shading and a clear legend can be read on a phone if the y-axis is labelled clearly.

chartExplainers.title: A shrinking share of the economy, but still two in five jobs
chartExplainers.takeaway: Agriculture’s share of GDP has fallen from 62% to 14%, but its share of employment has fallen only from 63% to 42%, leaving a wide gap.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines tell the story of farming in modern India. The dark line, agriculture’s share of gross value added, drops from 61.7% in 1951 to 13.8% in 2026. The light line, agriculture’s share of total employment, falls from 63.1% in 1991 to 41.6% in 2025. The gap between the two is the reason a drought is a small event for GDP and a large event for livelihoods. Over 40% of Indian workers still depend on farming for their income, and food takes a large share of poor households’ budgets. So even when the monsoon barely dents the growth rate, it still sets rural wages and the price of a meal for millions.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the human underpinning of the entire monsoon question. It shows how many people a bad rain year can reach.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The two lines start close together and then diverge. The wider the gap, the more employment exceeds output share.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The employment series begins in 1991, not 1951, due to data availability. The comparison with GVA is still valid.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A dual-line chart with contrasting colours and clear labels works on a phone if the gap is visually prominent.

sourceNotes: IMD Pune: all-India and regional southwest monsoon rainfall, 1901-2025.
NOAA: Oceanic Nino Index (ONI) and Dipole Mode Index (DMI), 1950-2025.
RBI DBIE: wholesale price indices and national accounts (agricultural GVA).
ICRISAT District Level Database: crop yields for kharif rice, coarse cereals, and other crops.
UPAg: Third Advance Estimates of crop production, 2025-26.
World Bank: employment in agriculture and CPI inflation.
caveats: All ocean indices are measured as anomalies from their respective base periods; rainfall departures are from the 1971-2020 LPA.
Correlations and averages are descriptive, not causal. They summarise historical behaviour and do not prove that El Nino alone caused any single year’s outcome.
The choropleth and crop-yield data are based on historical El Nino events (1966-2017) and do not include the most recent years.
Wholesale food inflation (WPI) is used for long-run consistency; it is not the retail price that consumers pay.
The 2026 monsoon forecast is a live projection and will be updated. This page uses data up to 2025 for historical analysis.

plannedCharts.title: A century and a quarter of monsoons that never sit still
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMD all-India June-September rainfall departure · 1901-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before El Nino enters the story, see the raw material: every monsoon since 1901, with no two alike.

plannedCharts.title: The same record as a line, so the swings are easy to follow
plannedCharts.subtitle: IMD all-India monsoon rainfall departure · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A line makes the booms, busts and recoveries clearer than stripes, and sets up the El Nino overlay that follows.

plannedCharts.title: On average, El Nino monsoons are the driest
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average rainfall departure by Pacific state · all-India · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The shortest honest answer to the question: sort every monsoon by the Pacific's state and average the rain.

plannedCharts.title: El Nino roughly doubles the odds of a weak monsoon
plannedCharts.subtitle: Share of each Pacific state's years that finished below normal · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Risk is a frequency, not a slogan - so count how often each Pacific state actually produced a weak monsoon.

plannedCharts.title: How you define 'an El Nino year' changes the headline
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average all-India rainfall under three El Nino definitions · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether El Nino looks mild or menacing depends on how strictly you count it - a choice most coverage hides. We show three definitions at once.

plannedCharts.title: Has the Pacific's grip on the monsoon loosened?
plannedCharts.subtitle: 21-year rolling correlation between Pacific warmth and India's rainfall · centred years 1960-2015
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Scientists have argued since the late 1990s that the El Nino-monsoon link is fraying. This tracks the link's strength decade by decade instead of asserting it.

plannedCharts.title: Two oceans, not one, set the monsoon's odds
plannedCharts.subtitle: Pacific ONI and Indian Ocean Dipole, monsoon-season averages · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India does not only watch the Pacific. The Indian Ocean Dipole right next door can push the monsoon the other way.

plannedCharts.title: Does the Indian Ocean rescue an El Nino monsoon?
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average rainfall in El Nino years, split by the Indian Ocean Dipole · 1950-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: If the second ocean really matters, El Nino years with a friendly Indian Ocean should look different from those without.

plannedCharts.title: The El Nino years that refused to follow the rule
plannedCharts.subtitle: El Nino monsoons that still finished near-normal or wet · rainfall departure
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: If the article stopped at the drought years it would mislead. These El Nino monsoons came out fine.

plannedCharts.title: ...and the El Nino droughts India still remembers
plannedCharts.subtitle: The deepest all-India rainfall deficits among El Nino monsoons
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: These are the benchmarks 2026 will be measured against if the season turns.

plannedCharts.title: Same El Nino, very different Indias
plannedCharts.subtitle: Rainfall departure in El Nino years, by IMD region
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The national average hides the geography that actually decides crops and incomes.

plannedCharts.title: El Nino hits the northwest hardest
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average rainfall departure in El Nino monsoons, by region · 17 events
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Some regions lean on the monsoon far more than others. This ranks who loses the most rain when the Pacific warms.

plannedCharts.title: When in the season the rain goes missing
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average monthly rainfall departure, El Nino monsoons vs a typical year · all-India
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Timing decides damage: a dry July at sowing hurts more than a dry, post-harvest September. So we split the season by month.

plannedCharts.title: Where El Nino actually cuts the rice harvest
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average kharif rice yield change in El Nino monsoons, by state · ICRISAT district data · 1966-2017
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The rainfall map and the harvest map are not the same picture - this puts El Nino's actual yield mark on the states.

plannedCharts.title: Why the rainfall map is not the yield map
plannedCharts.unit: % above or below the recent 5-year normal
plannedCharts.subtitle: El Nino-year yield vs the crop's own prior 5-year average: irrigated rice vs rainfed coarse cereals, by region · ICRISAT · 1966-2017
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The northwest loses the most monsoon rain, yet its rice barely flinches. Splitting an irrigated crop from a rainfed one shows why.

plannedCharts.title: Which crops El Nino actually hits
plannedCharts.unit: % above or below the crop's recent 5-year normal
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average El Nino-year yield vs the crop's own prior 5-year normal, all-India · ICRISAT · 1966-2017
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: If irrigation is the shield, the damage should sort cleanly by crop. It does.

plannedCharts.title: Rainfall still shows up in the harvest
plannedCharts.subtitle: Correlation of monsoon rainfall with crop output · DES panel · 1950-51 to 2024-25
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The monsoon matters because, decades of irrigation later, it is still visible in the crop record.

plannedCharts.title: Farm output feels the Pacific - but less than you would guess
plannedCharts.subtitle: Average real agriculture GVA growth by Pacific state · RBI national accounts · 1951-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: If El Nino reaches the wider economy, it should show up in how fast farm output grows. RBI's national accounts let us check.

plannedCharts.title: The food buffer India carries into the 2026 risk season
plannedCharts.subtitle: Production estimates for major crops · UPAg 2025-26 Third Advance Estimates
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether a weak 2026 monsoon becomes a price shock depends partly on the stockpile the country starts with.

plannedCharts.title: A drought does not automatically mean dearer food
plannedCharts.subtitle: Post-monsoon (Oct-Dec) wholesale food inflation in El Nino years · RBI WPI · 1982-2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The longest food-price record we have - wholesale prices back to 1982 - lets us test the El-Nino-to-inflation link across eight events, not two.

plannedCharts.title: After a drought, food prices do not move as one
plannedCharts.subtitle: Post-monsoon wholesale inflation by food group, four El Nino droughts · RBI WPI
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: 'Food inflation' is really a bundle of separate shocks. Splitting it shows which foods actually carry the pain.

plannedCharts.title: El Nino is one driver of food prices, not the only one
plannedCharts.subtitle: Headline, wholesale-food and retail-food inflation, with El Nino monsoon years shaded · 1960-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The long view is the honest test of the El Nino-to-prices idea: if the monsoon drove inflation, the spikes would line up with the shaded El Nino years. Mostly they do not.

plannedCharts.title: A shrinking share of the economy, but still two in five jobs
plannedCharts.subtitle: Agriculture's share of India's output and of its workforce · 1951-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the human heart of the monsoon question: how many people a bad rain year can actually reach, set against how little it now moves headline GDP.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.

furtherReading: Davis, M. (2001). Late Victorian Holocausts: El Nino Famines and the Making of the Third World. Verso. On the 1876-78 and 1896-1902 El Nino famines in India.
Walker, G. T. (1924). Correlation in seasonal variations of weather, IX. Memoirs of the India Meteorological Department. The paper that named the Southern Oscillation.
Bjerknes, J. (1969). Atmospheric teleconnections from the equatorial Pacific. Monthly Weather Review, 97(3). Linked the ocean warming (El Nino) to the Southern Oscillation.
Kumar, K. K., Rajagopalan, B. & Cane, M. A. (1999). On the weakening relationship between the Indian monsoon and ENSO. Science, 284.

Kumar, K. K., Rajagopalan, B., Hoerling, M., Bates, G. & Cane, M. (2006). Unraveling the mystery of Indian monsoon failure during El Nino. Science, 314. On why central-Pacific (Modoki) El Ninos hurt the monsoon more.
Ashok, K., Behera, S. K., Rao, S. A., Weng, H. & Yamagata, T. (2007). El Nino Modoki and its possible teleconnection. Journal of Geophysical Research, 112.

Parthasarathy, B., Munot, A. A. & Kothawale, D. R. (1994). All-India monthly and seasonal rainfall series: 1871-1993. Theoretical and Applied Climatology. The homogeneous monsoon-rainfall record (IITM, Pune).
ICRISAT District Level Database (apportioned), 1966-2017, data.icrisat.org. District-level crop area, production and yield used for the regional harvest maps.
